terms.definition: Disciplic succession; unbroken chain of transmission, like git history preserving truth across generations.
Received; obtained through transmission, not self-generated.
Saintly kings; rulers established in dharma, the executive branch of spiritual order.
They knew; direct perception, not mere theory.
In the course of great time; entropy affects even the strongest lineages without maintenance.
Scattered, lost; access severed, though truth itself is never destroyed.
terms.roots: from naś (to be lost, to perish)

terms.definition: O scorcher of enemies; Arjuna's epithet indicating capacity to burn through obstacles.
terms.roots: from para (enemy) + tap (to burn, to heat)

terms.definition: Whenever and wherever; the cyclical trigger condition indicating divine intervention is not random but responds to systematic degradation.
Decline, depletion; the state when dharma has fallen below operational threshold.
terms.roots: from glā (to fade, to be exhausted)

terms.definition: Rise, predominance; the upsurge of irreligion's influence over consciousness.

terms.type: first person singular present causative of sṛj
terms.definition: I manifest, I emit; not creation but making apparent what was always present.
Myself; the Personal Absolute, not impersonal force but conscious descent.
Complete surrender, devotion as orientation of the total being toward Īśvara.
Approaching by bowing down; complete surrender, prostration before the spiritual master. The necessary humility that opens the door to what lies beyond personal capacity.
terms.roots: from pra (intensive prefix) + ni (down) + pat (to fall)

terms.definition: Inquiry from all sides; submissive questioning aimed at understanding, not debate. Intelligent querying of the system — read the documentation, ask specific questions, accept the output.
Service; selfless action rendered without desire for result. The method by which knowledge is digested through direct application.
terms.roots: from sev (to serve, attend to, honor)

terms.definition: One who sees the truth; the self-realized soul who perceives reality as it is, not as it appears. The one who sees the essence beneath the form.
terms.roots: from tattva (the essence, truth) + darśin (one who sees)

terms.definition: Instruction; the act of sitting near. The guru points to what is already present but unrecognized. Transmission of realized knowledge from one who has seen.

Imperishable, inexhaustible. The soul's fundamental quality: it cannot be diminished by birth, confounded by death, or fragmented across time. Like root access to the system — credentials that persist across reboots.
terms.roots: from a (privative prefix) + vya (to decay, perish)

terms.definition: Subduer of the enemy. Arjuna's epithet carries weight here. The enemy to be subdued is not the Kurus on the battlefield — it is the ignorance that makes him forget his nature, his past, his purpose. The warrior who can defeat external armies cannot defeat internal darkness without divine knowledge.
terms.roots: from param (greatest, beyond) + tapa (to heat, to burn)

terms.definition: Birth. In classical Sāṅkhya: not the origin of the soul (which is eternal), but the assumption of a new instrument — the body-mind complex — through which puruṣa falsely identifies as agent and enjoyer. The phala (fruit) of prakṛti's cycle, binding until knowledge dissolves the bond.
terms.roots: from jan (to be born, to arise)

terms.definition: Prescribed, regulated, bound by duty. Dharma-specific action aligned with one's position; hacker metaphor: The code compiled for your specific architecture.
terms.roots: from ni (down, into) + yam (to restrain, regulate)

terms.definition: Action, work, performance of duty. From root kr (to do); hacker metaphor: The executable—action that produces output in the system.
Without action, inaction. State of avoiding duty through laziness, fear, or false renunciation; hacker metaphor: A process stopped but still consuming resources.
The yoga of action; Patanjali's term (YS II.1) for the path integrating tapas, svādhyāya, and īśvara-praṇidhānāna.
The fierce, intense, transformative. The yoga indicating purifying fire energy favorable for decisive action and burning away impurities.
terms.roots: from rudra (the fierce/howler, name for Shiva)

terms.definition: Detachment: Operating without the hooks of expectation. Recognition that the actor and the fruits are in different categories, even while the action continues.
terms.roots: from saj (to cling, attach) with privative a-

terms.definition: Proper execution: Not mere doing, but calibrated action aligned with dharma and timing. The integration of movement toward rightful completion.
The Supreme: For the bhakta, the Personality of Godhead; for the jñānī, liberation. The destination reached when attachment to results is traded for connection to source.

Obstacle yoga: An inauspicious combination meaning 'falling away.' The prompt to act with extra care, as wrong action now carries amplified consequence. Not an excuse for paralysis, but a call to precision.
not disturbed, undisturbed; lit. un-struck, un-violated — in Sāṅkhya terms, the puruṣa (conscious witness) remains unaffected by the perpetual injections of prakṛtic activity
terms.roots: from pāpa (strike) + viddha (struck) with privative a-

terms.definition: being filled continuously — the ocean's volume is maintained by ceaseless input. Unlike the kāmī (desirer) who empties with indulgence, the devotee receives without depletion
steady foundation, immovable base — acala (unmoving) + pratiṣṭha (standing firm). The root access of consciousness that no prakṛtic process can revoke
ocean, gathering-place of waters — from sam (together) + udra (water). The field containing all phenomena without being modified by them
mastery, control — from vaś (to be subject to). Not the will-to-power of forceful domination, but the effortless sovereignty of the full
terms.roots: from vaś (to wish, to be subject to) + kāra (making)

terms.definition: peace, tranquility, cessation of agitation — not the absence of stimulus but the presence of completion. The goal of Sāṅkhya and the fruit of yoga
you must tolerate/endure — active forbearance, not passive suffering; the austere patience that maintains practice through all conditions
sense-object contacts — the interface where consciousness meets prakṛti; the API boundary where input triggers response
impermanent, non-eternal — the defining quality of all prakṛtic phenomena; that which cannot be relied upon for lasting satisfaction
those that come and go — describing sensations as temporary visitors, not permanent residents
cold and heat — the classic paired opposites; all dvandva that disturb the undiscriminating mind
O son of Kuntī — appellation invoking Arjuna's maternal lineage; reminder of the duty that comes from noble birth
O descendant of Bharata — appellation invoking Arjuna's paternal lineage; reminder of the greatness of the Bharata dynasty
